scenarios.question: When caring for pediatric patients, the nurse should:
options: Exclude family members from care decisions
Use the same approach as with adult patients
Types of Interventions Individual Family therapy Behavior modification
Focus only on the child's physical symptoms
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is Types of Interventions Individual Family therapy Behavior modification. Types of Interventions Individual Family therapy Behavior modification. Family-centered, age-appropriate care is essential in pediatrics. (Slide 4)

scenarios.question: When caring for pediatric patients, the nurse should:
options: Include family in care decisions and use age-appropriate communication
Focus only on the child's medical diagnosis
Treat all children the same as adults
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is Include family in care decisions and use age-appropriate communication. Family-centered care is essential. Include caregivers, use age-appropriate language, consider developmental needs. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Pain assessment in non-verbal children uses:
options: Asking the child to describe pain
FLACC scale (Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability)
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is Numeric rating scale (0-10). FLACC is validated for infants/young children. FACES for 3+ years. Numeric for 8+ years. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: The most reliable indicator of pain in infants is:
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is Parental report. Infants: observe behavioral cues (facial expression, body posture, cry quality). FLACC scale. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Which vital sign changes first in a deteriorating pediatric patient?
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is Blood pressure. Respiratory rate changes first in children (compensatory mechanism). BP is a late sign of decompensation. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is the correct pediatric CPR ratio?
options: 30:2 for single rescuer, 15:2 for two rescuers
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is 30:1. Infant/child CPR: 30:2 single, 15:2 two rescuers. Compression depth: 1/3 chest depth. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: The leading cause of death in children over 1 year is:
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is Unintentional injuries. Unintentional injuries (drowning, MVAs, burns) are #1 cause of death in children >1 year. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: When should car seats face rearward?
options: Until age 2 or until child exceeds weight/height limits
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is Until age 8. AAP: rear-facing until age 2 or until exceeding car seat limits. Then forward-facing with harness. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is the recommended daily water intake for a 5-year-old?
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is Unlimited. Children 4-8 years: ~5 cups/day fluids. More with activity/heat. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Which is the best way to prevent childhood obesity?
options: Healthy diet and regular physical activity
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is Healthy diet and regular physical activity. Prevention: balanced diet, 60 min/day physical activity, limit screen time, family meals. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: A child who is choking and cannot cough or speak needs:
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is Nothing — wait. Choking: abdominal thrusts for conscious child >1 year. For infant: 5 back blows + 5 chest thrusts. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: The safest car seat position for infants is:
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is Any position. Infants: rear-facing until age 2 or until exceeding car seat limits. Back seat only. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: When should children transition to a booster seat?
options: When they outgrow forward-facing harness (typically 4-8 years)
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is When they outgrow forward-facing harness (typically 4-8 years). Booster: when child exceeds forward-facing harness limits (~40-65 lbs). Until seat belt fits properly (usually 8-12 years). (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Drowning prevention includes:
options: Fencing pools, close supervision, CPR training
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is Only supervision. Drowning prevention: 4-sided fencing, constant supervision, CPR training, water safety education. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Lead poisoning prevention includes:
options: Hand washing, removing paint chips, iron-rich diet
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is Hand washing, removing paint chips, iron-rich diet. Lead: hand washing, wet mopping, removing peeling paint, iron/calcium-rich diet. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Poison prevention: keep medications:
options: Locked up and in original containers
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is Locked up and in original containers. Poison prevention: locked cabinets, original containers, childproof caps, poison control number posted. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: The leading cause of death in children 1-14 years is:
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is Congenital anomalies. Unintentional injuries (MVAs, drowning, burns, poisoning) are #1 cause of death in children. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Bike helmet use should begin:
options: When child starts riding a bike
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is At age 5. Bike helmet: required for all ages. Proper fit: level on head, snug straps. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Match each developmental theorist with their key concept.
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is Option 1. Erikson: psychosocial crises. Piaget: cognitive development. Kohlberg: moral reasoning. (Slide 15-20)

scenarios.question: Complete the statement about pediatric care.
scenarios.cloze_text: Pediatric nursing requires [[BLANK1]] care and consideration of [[BLANK2]] in all interactions.
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is Option 1. Family-centered, age-appropriate care is fundamental in pediatrics. (Slide 10)

scenarios.question: Click on the normal developmental findings.
scenarios.highlight_text: The child demonstrates age-appropriate milestones. Growth follows expected percentiles. The child engages with caregivers appropriately.
scenarios.rationale: The correct answers are Option age-appropriate milestones1, Option Growth follows expected percentiles1, Option engages with caregivers appropriately1. All are normal pediatric findings. (Slide 25)

scenarios.question: A child presents with concerning developmental findings.
scenarios.rationale: The correct answer is Option 1. Early identification and family support are key. (Slide 30)
